summary: The author secured 4th place in the MDC competition by developing a pipeline for candidate extraction and primary/secondary citation type classification of dataset mentions in scientific papers. They prioritized high-recall candidate retrieval using the DCC and PMC corpora, supplemented by regex and LLM filtering for edge cases. To overcome a small and noisy training set, they generated synthetic labels via a tool-calling agent on Europe PMC data, warmed up Qwen 2.5 models, and fine-tuned them with masked dataset ID augmentations. Inference utilized a cascaded routing strategy across Qwen 2.5-14B, 32B, and 72B models, combined with EMA checkpoint averaging and heuristic post-processing rules.
extensive_summary:
## Candidate Extraction
The pipeline began by retrieving candidate DOI and accession mentions from external corpora: the Data Citation Corpus v3.0 (DCC) and the PMC TextMinedTerms corpus. The authors filtered out known false-positive repositories (e.g., `czi`, `gca`, `hgnc`, `figshare`) and retained only candidates explicitly present in the PDF/XML article text. While they initially relied on a regex fallback followed by LLM filtering to handle multi-line PDF artifacts, they abandoned this approach after discovering that the DCC and PMC corpora already provided near-perfect recall with high precision.

## Type Classification & Training
Training robust classifiers proved difficult due to the small and noisy competition dataset. The team implemented a tool-calling agent that leveraged keyword and semantic search on the Europe PMC open access subset to gather contextual evidence and generate synthetic labels. They warmed up public Qwen 2.5 family models on this synthetic dataset before fine-tuning on the competition examples. To improve model diversity in the ensemble, they also trained additional models by injecting pseudo-labeled examples generated by Qwen-2.5-72B into the training mix. Data augmentation involved masking dataset IDs with `<mask_i>` tokens, and they capped dataset mentions at a maximum of 24 per article to prevent high-count articles from disproportionately skewing the fine-tuning datamix. Checkpoints were averaged using Exponential Moving Average (EMA).

## Context Construction & Inference
Context windows were carefully engineered to maximize relevant information, combining the first 1400 characters (as a metadata proxy), snippets containing the target dataset IDs, data availability sections, text snippets with other DOI mentions, and table headers/footers for long tables. During inference, the team applied heuristic rules, such as assuming secondary type for specific accession IDs (`cath`, `alphafold`, etc.) and removing accession IDs when DOI mentions appeared in data availability sections or when primary DOI probabilities exceeded 0.8. A cascaded inference strategy routed initial predictions from a fine-tuned Qwen-2.5-14B model, sent uncertain examples (50% of DOI cases + 20% of accession cases) to a Qwen-2.5-32B model, and finally routed the top 10% most difficult predictions to a Qwen-2.5-72B model.
competition_flow: Raw scientific paper texts were processed to extract candidate DOI and accession mentions using external corpora and regex fallbacks, followed by context window construction, LLM fine-tuning with synthetic and pseudo labels, cascaded model inference, and heuristic post-processing to produce final primary/secondary classifications.
data_processing: Filtered DCC and PMC corpora by removing specific repositories (e.g., `czi`, `gca`, `hgnc`, `figshare`)
Retained only mentions explicitly present in PDF/XML article text
Capped dataset mentions at a maximum of 24 per article to prevent high-count articles from skewing training
Masked dataset IDs with `<mask_i>` tokens for data augmentation
Constructed context windows combining the first 1400 characters, mention snippets, data availability sections, other DOI mentions, and table headers/footers
ensembling: Models were ensembled by averaging checkpoints using Exponential Moving Average (EMA) and training separate models with pseudo-labeled examples for diversity, while inference employed a cascaded routing strategy based on prediction uncertainty and probability thresholds.
insights: Synthetic labels generated via a tool-calling agent significantly improved training on small and noisy datasets.
Composing context windows with structured metadata proxies, mention snippets, and data availability sections maximized relevant information for classification.
Limiting the number of dataset mentions per article prevented high-count articles from disproportionately influencing model training.
critical_findings: The DCC and PMC corpora provided near-perfect recall with high precision, rendering the initial regex fallback approach largely unnecessary.
Specific accession IDs like `cath` and `alphafold` were predominantly secondary citations, allowing safe assumption during inference.
Articles containing 32+ accession IDs had an outsized impact on training, necessitating a hard cap of 24 mentions per article.
what_did_not_work: The original regex-based candidate extraction approach was abandoned after discovering that the DCC and PMC corpora offered near-perfect recall with high precision.
